specification.topic-statement: The format buffer accumulates formatted text incrementally and provides access to the assembled result as a null-terminated string.

scope.in-scope: Creating a format buffer, appending text via printf-style formatting or direct string append, inserting indentation, retrieving the accumulated string and its length, and applying a line-and-character truncation limit when appending content.
scope.boundary: The format buffer stores its content in an underlying resizable byte array. This topic drives growth through appends but does not manage the byte array's internal layout.
Callers create the buffer, append to it, then retrieve the final string. The returned string pointer remains valid for the lifetime of the caller-provided allocation context.

field.name: underlying byte array
field.note: resizes automatically as content is appended

field.name: allocation context
field.note: the talloc context provided at creation

behavior.creation:
A new format buffer is created under a caller-provided allocation context. The internal byte array is initialized with a 32-byte growth increment. The buffer begins empty. If any allocation fails during creation, the process terminates immediately.
The allocation context is stored internally and used when the accumulated string is later retrieved.
behavior.printf-style-append:
A format string and variadic arguments are rendered to a temporary buffer using standard printf-style formatting. The byte count required for the formatted output is determined first; then the formatted bytes are appended one at a time to the internal byte array.
If the printf rendering fails (format string error or output truncation detected), an error is returned. The buffer's existing content is not modified on error.
If any internal memory allocation fails, the process terminates immediately.
behavior.string-append: A null-terminated string is appended to the internal byte array one byte at a time. An empty string is a no-op that returns success without modifying the buffer. If any internal memory allocation fails, the process terminates immediately.
behavior.indentation: A non-negative indentation level is specified. That many space characters (0x20) are appended to the buffer. A level of zero is a no-op. Negative indentation levels are not accepted (the caller must ensure the value is non-negative). If any internal memory allocation fails, the process terminates immediately.
behavior.string-retrieval:
The accumulated content is returned as a null-terminated string. If the last byte in the buffer is not already a null terminator, one is appended before returning. The returned pointer points into the buffer's internal storage and remains valid until the buffer's allocation context is freed.
Retrieving the string is not a pure read operation. If the buffer does not yet end with a null terminator, this operation appends one, permanently modifying the buffer's byte array. Subsequent retrievals detect the null terminator already present and return without further modification.
behavior.length-retrieval: The number of content bytes in the buffer is returned, excluding any trailing null terminator. If the last byte is a null terminator, the reported length is one less than the internal array size. Otherwise the length equals the internal array size. This operation does not modify the buffer.
behavior.truncated-append:
Content up to a combined limit of 3 lines and 400 characters is appended to the buffer. The content is scanned byte by byte:
- Each newline (0x0A) increments the line count. If the line count exceeds 3, scanning stops at the newline position. - Each byte increments the character count. If the character count reaches 400, scanning stops after that byte.
If scanning stopped before the end of the content, only the bytes up to the stopping point are appended, followed by "..." to indicate truncation. If the entire content fits within the limits, it is appended in full without the truncation indicator.
If the content length is zero, the literal text "(no output)" is appended instead of the content.
